core_behavior: The system must ensure that data remains consistent across all components, modules, and interfaces.
The system will maintain data integrity, accuracy, and validity throughout the system's lifecycle.
The system will achieve data consistency by implementing a combination of data validation, normalization, and synchronization mechanisms.
inputs: User-generated data (e.g., form submissions, file uploads)
Data from external sources (e.g., APIs, databases, file systems)
System-generated data (e.g., logs, metrics, audit trails)
outputs: Validated and normalized data stored in the system's database or data storage
Data transmitted to external systems or interfaces (e.g., APIs, messaging queues)
Reports, logs, and audit trails that reflect consistent and accurate data
assumptions: The system will use a centralized data storage mechanism (e.g., relational database, NoSQL database) to store and manage data.
Data validation will be performed at multiple layers, including client-side, server-side, and database-level validation.
The system will implement data normalization techniques (e.g., data formatting, data transformation) to ensure consistent data representation.
The system will use transactions or locking mechanisms to ensure data consistency during concurrent updates or modifications.
The system will perform regular data backups and maintain a change history to facilitate data recovery and auditing.
failure_modes: Data validation errors: The system will reject invalid data and provide error messages or feedback to the user.

Data inconsistencies: The system will detect and correct data inconsistencies through automated mechanisms (e.g., data reconciliation, data repair) or manual intervention (e.g., administrator correction).
System crashes or downtime: The system will maintain data consistency by using transactions, locking mechanisms, or other fault-tolerant techniques to prevent data corruption or loss.
edge_cases: Null or empty data inputs: The system will handle null or empty data inputs by either rejecting them or using default values.
Duplicate data: The system will detect and prevent duplicate data entries through unique identifiers or data normalization techniques.
Data type mismatches: The system will handle data type mismatches by performing data type conversions or rejecting invalid data.
Concurrent updates: The system will handle concurrent updates by using transactions, locking mechanisms, or other concurrency control techniques.
security_constraints: Data encryption: The system will encrypt sensitive data both in transit (e.g., HTTPS) and at rest (e.g., encrypted storage).
Access control: The system will implement role-based access control, authentication, and authorization mechanisms to restrict data access to authorized users.
Data backups: The system will perform regular data backups and store them securely to prevent data loss or tampering.
Audit trails: The system will maintain detailed audit trails to track data changes, updates, and access.
performance_constraints: Data processing throughput: The system will process data inputs and updates within a reasonable time frame (e.g., < 1 second) to ensure timely data consistency.
Data storage capacity: The system will have sufficient data storage capacity to handle expected data volumes and growth.
System scalability: The system will be designed to scale horizontally or vertically to handle increased data volumes, user traffic, or system load.
Data retrieval latency: The system will retrieve data within a reasonable time frame (e.g., < 100 ms) to ensure responsive system performance.
